storm.summary_layperson: No storms in the area — just a chance of light drizzle from the northerly flow, with no thunder or lightning risk today.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Sound water (69°F) and air temperature are nearly equal today, so Long Island Sound's usual storm-dampening effect is not active.
storm.detail_html:
The regional picture is quiet. KOKX radar shows no storm cells — no reflectivity detected, nothing approaching. Instability is negligible: HRRR CAPE is 30 J/kg at the current hour, effectively zero for convective purposes.

The NWS forecast carries a 30% chance of light rain this afternoon, reflecting the moist northerly flow rather than any convective development. Any precipitation reaching the cove today would be stratiform — light drizzle or a brief shower, not thunderstorms. No NWS alerts are in effect.

The OKX Area Forecast Discussion references 1,000–1,500 J/kg of SBCAPE as a concern for Thursday and Friday, not today. Today's atmosphere is stable and benign.

The Long Island Sound protective mechanism is inactive. Water temperature (69°F, Bridgeport Harbor) matches air temperature (70°F) within 1°F — the coastal stabilization module scores this as marine layer absent. There is no Sound-effect storm deflection today, but also nothing to deflect. The elevated CAPE risk for Thursday/Friday will need a fresh evaluation when those days arrive.

wind.regime_label: Gradient NNE — offshore, no thermal driver
wind.tactics_blurb: Northerly offshore flow arrives puffy and oscillating — expect 10–20° swings around the NNE mean and a gradual speed drop from ~9 kt at 5 PM to 5–6 kt by 9 PM. Stay center to cover both sides of the course; tack on headers as the angles cycle.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime B-moderate (gradient-only). The land–water temperature differential is only +0.7°F (inland air 70°F, Sound water 69°F) — well below the 5°F threshold needed for a thermally-driven sea breeze. All coastal and inland stations are pointing N–NNE, and ensemble sea-breeze detection shows zero southerly flow anywhere in the network. This is pure gradient wind with no thermal contribution.

HRRR 19Z forecasts NNE 8–9 kt at 17:00 local, softening to 5–6 kt by 21:00. RYC WeatherLink confirms the regime: N 10.7 kt gusting 16.1 kt right now — the model is aligned with current observations. Authoritative-site mean speed is 9.6 kt; direction agreement across 26 usable stations is 0.77 (coherent flow). Note: KBDR and KHVN anemometers are both flagged down, reducing coastal METAR wind coverage, but RYC provides reliable in-cove ground truth.

For the cove, northerly flow arrives from over land — the classic puffy, oscillating regime. Expect cycles of 10–20° around the NNE mean (roughly 0–35°) as gusts roll off terrain. The direction is NNE (~16–22°) rather than true NW, giving it a slight laminar quality on the NE component — less violently shifty than a pure NW day, but nothing like the persistent clean flow of a SSW sea breeze.

In Regime B-moderate, wind doesn't collapse at sunset the way a thermal sea breeze does. The softening from ~9 kt at 5 PM to 5–6 kt by 9 PM reflects daytime thermal mixing tapering after the ~20:19 sunset, not a regime transition. Direction holds N–NNE throughout. After 21:00, the boundary layer stabilizes and gusts smooth out, but speed will be light (4–6 kt) — racing gets drifty by 10 PM.

Buoy 44040 is stale (105 min old), so the over-water wind reading is unavailable for cross-check. For this northerly regime, that matters less than on a sea-breeze day — the flow originates from land, not across the Sound. RYC and the APRS spatial ensemble are the reliable reads today.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 stale — last reading 105 min ago; over-water wind and SST unavailable
KBDR and KHVN anemometers both flagged down — coastal METAR wind coverage degraded
HRRR hourly data does not include gust values; gusts estimated from current RYC gust/speed ratio
